headline: Kashmir's Lakes Rise Again Through Community Action
meta_description: Conservationist Manzoor Ahmad Wangnoo leads community-driven revival of Kashmir's degraded lakes and wetlands after decades of decline.
summary_short: After nearly half of Kashmir's lakes disappeared, one conservationist and his community are proving restoration is possible through collective effort.
body:
When Manzoor Ahmad Wangnoo returned to Kashmir in the early 2000s, he found Nigeen Lake choked with polythene bags and dead animals. Illegal encroachment had fenced off sections of the water body for private use. But rather than accept this decline, Wangnoo gathered friends and neighbors, and together they cleaned the lake in phases. Today, he says, you cannot find a single piece of polythene floating in it.

His work reveals a harsh reality: nearly half of the 697 lakes recorded across Jammu and Kashmir in the 1960s have vanished, while another 203 have shrunk. Decades of pollution, encroachment and unplanned urbanization have ravaged freshwater ecosystems that once sustained the region's biodiversity, agriculture and tourism.

Through the Nigeen Lake Conservation Organisation and its Mission Ehsaas program, Wangnoo has mobilized residents and government agencies to restore degraded water bodies, including the Khushalsar-Gilsar wetland system in Srinagar's heart. The word Ehsaas—meaning awareness in Urdu and Kashmiri—captures his philosophy: conservation is about reestablishing our relationship with nature.

Wangnoo's two decades of work show what's possible when communities take responsibility. People who once treated lakes like dustbins now understand these water bodies are theirs to protect. The transformation of Nigeen Lake stands as proof that degraded ecosystems can heal—if we commit to the work together.
